title: TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure
summary:
In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”. The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026. TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has announced its new regional operating model. Image courtesy Penfolds

In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”.

The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026.

TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

 

Market depletions

The company has seen “improved depletions performance in 3Q26”, noting “strong depletions growth” in China “driven by the ongoing demand for Bin 389 and Bin 407”. TWE said that performance through the Chinese New Year period was a highlight, with “depletions up 40% in China on a seasonally adjusted basis and momentum continuing to the end of 3Q26”.

In ANZ, 3Q26 depletions grew 11% and in Asia (excluding China), depletions grew 14% on a seasonally adjusted basis according to TWE.

Treasury Americas overall US depletions grew 9.1% in 3Q26, “reflecting improved momentum”.

The winery said that the positive depletions momentum is expected to support its focus on reducing customer inventory levels in China and the US.

 

Funding update

TWE said that as a part of its ongoing funding program, it has “established new debt commitments” totalling $300 million “from a number of lenders within its global banking group”. The company said these commitments will be used to refinance F27 debt maturities and further increase TWE’s liquidity position, which it claims is expected to exceed $1 billion by the end of F26. The winery said it retains “significant headroom to the financial covenants under its borrowing arrangements and remains confident in its ability to return leverage to the target 1.5-2.0x range”.

TWE CEO Sam Fischer said that the company is reshaping to drive clearer accountability for performance and to enable faster, more market-connected decision-making.

“Combining the deep local insight of our in-market teams with the scale and expertise of our global functions will step change in-market execution, whilst retaining our enhanced focus on Penfolds and other priority luxury brands.

“I am pleased with the progress we are making on elevating our focus on depletions performance across our key markets, and we remain focused on continuing the improved momentum,” he said.

 

‘Executive Leadership Team’ changes

TWE will realign its ‘Executive Leadership Team’ (ELT), driving the organisation transition to the new operating model, effective 1 October 2026.

Tom King, who is currently managing director for Penfolds, will assume a new position as chief commercial officer. He will report to CEO Sam Fischer and lead the regional sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities for all regions except the Americas. He will be based in Melbourne.

Reporting to Tom King, Angus Lilley, who is currently managing director for Treasury Collective, will lead the ANZ and Europe regions “with accountability for regional sales and marketing activities” for TWE’s focused brand portfolio from Melbourne.

Jack Wu, currently general manager Mainland China for Penfolds, will lead the Greater China region, based in Shanghai.

Kirsty Keyte, current chief marketing officer for Penfolds, will become the new position of chief marketing and innovation officer. She will be based in Melbourne and report to Tom King, being responsible for global marketing and the delivery of global brand strategy.

Also reporting to Fischer will be Ben Dollard, current president of Treasury Americas. He will lead TWE’s Americas region with responsibility for sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities.

“The direct CEO reporting line reflects heightened focus on TWE’s Americas business, providing direct line of sight into in-market execution in the Americas. A dotted reporting line into the CCO ensures a consistent and disciplined execution framework across all regions including the Americas,” said TWE.

Kerrin Petty will remain chief supply and sustainability officer, with an expanded global supply remit, including Americas supply. Petty will report to Fischer and be based in Adelaide.

Group functions reporting to CEO Fischer include the chief financial and strategy officer, chief legal and corporate relations officer (incorporating the legal, corporate affairs and company secretariat functions), chief people officer and chief digital and technology officer.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

 

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.

title: ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research
summary:
A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards. Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”. A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

The post ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Yinlin Bai, the PhD candidate leading the pest research. Image supplied

A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards.

Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”.

A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

Wine Australia described how scale insects and mealybugs can damage grapevines by feeding on plant sap, which can reduce vine vigour and slow growth. As the pests feed, they excrete honeydew that coats leaves and grape bunches, encouraging sooty mould which reduces photosynthesis and contaminates fruit.

This contamination can be devastating, leading to downgraded or rejected grapes at harvest. Scale insects and mealybugs are also known vectors of grapevine viruses, further compounding their potential impact on vine health and productivity.

Despite their impact, Wine Australia said that many aspects of their biology, ecology and movement across vineyards remain poorly understood.

“Knowing exactly which pests are present will help improve understanding of how they behave in vineyards and support the development of more precise and effective biological control strategies,” explained Dr Paul Smith, general manager research and innovation at Wine Australia.

Bai said that she is extremely grateful to Wine Australia and honoured to be receiving the award alongside her PhD top up scholarship.

“With the guidance of my collaborators, I hope to provide the Australian wine industry with science-based strategies to manage these challenging pests more effectively,” she said.

Seven candidates, including Bai, have also received Wine Australia top-up scholarships in 2026.

An additional project-based PhD scholarship has also been awarded to Jiaoying Qiao (Ying) for his research into the use of artificial intelligence in advisory services, which is being carried out in collaboration with the One Basin Cooperative Research Centre.

“Maintaining strong research capability is essential to the long-term success of the Australian wine sector.

“This round of scholarships supports emerging researchers whose work will address real world challenges for grapegrowers and winemakers, while strengthening collaboration between the sector and our leading research institutions.”

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research appeared first on Winetitles.
